label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out if an 8x8 chessboard with two diagonally opposite corners removed can be completely covered by 31 dominoes. Each domino covers two adjacent squares, right? Let me start by visualizing the chessboard. A standard chessboard has 8 rows and 8 columns, making 64 squares. If we remove two diagonally opposite corners, that leaves us with 62 squares. Since each domino covers two squares, 31 dominoes would indeed cover 62 squares. So the numbers add up. But that doesn't necessarily mean it's possible—there might be some other constraint. I remember something about domino tiling and colorings. Chessboards are alternately colored black and white. Each domino, when placed on the board, must cover one black and one white square. If that's the case, then for the entire board to be tileable by dominoes, the number of black and white squares must be equal. Let me check if removing two diagonally opposite corners affects that balance. In a standard chessboard, there are 32 black and 32 white squares. The corners are all the same color, right? Because on a standard chessboard, the corners are all one color. Let me confirm: the bottom-left corner is, say, black. Then moving to the right, the squares alternate, so the next corner (bottom-right) would be white if the board is even-sized? Wait, no. Wait, 8 is even. So each row starts with a different color. Wait, first square is black, then white, etc. So for an 8x8 board, each row has 4 black and 4 white squares. But the corners of each row would be the same color as the first square. So the four corners of the entire board: bottom-left, bottom-right, top-left, top-right. Since the board has even dimensions, the bottom-left and top-left are the same color (black, let's say), and the bottom-right and top-right are the same color (white).
